perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the preparation of a computer and related ------------- hardware and integration of components into a computer system; provided -------- that the term "Configuration" shall not include installation of a computer or related hardware at a customer site.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the basic setup of software on a computer. -------------
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Configuration" was comprehensive, including hardware and software integration, and explicitly excluded on-site installation. The modified definition is narrowed to just the basic software setup. This creates an in-text contradiction if other sections of the contract rely on the broader, original definition, especially regarding restrictions on competition related to 'Configuration'. This change affects the enforcement of Article III, which references 'Configuration' in the context of the Product Business. Specifically, section 3.1(a)(ii) referring to 'image load/configurations to such Service Account'. Now, whether certain activities fall under 'Configuration' becomes ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of "Configuration" from encompassing hardware and software integration to solely the basic software setup. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which references 'image load/configurations' in the context of services provided to customers. The original definition would include more comprehensive services, while the narrowed definition might exclude them, making it unclear whether the seller is restricted from certain activities under the non-competition agreement. The original text and explanation provided make it clear that the narrowed definition is at odds with the broader restriction on 'Configuration' found in the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, ------------------------------------- system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis, are not Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means services provided to clients. -------------------------------------
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Outsourcing and Professional Services" provided a clear distinction between high-end and lower-end services and specified pricing structures. The modified definition is extremely vague, defining it only as 'services provided to clients'. This omission creates an in-text contradiction because Article III refers to specific types of services within 'Outsourcing and Professional Services'. The lack of specificity makes it difficult to enforce the non-compete clause because it's unclear what activities are actually restricted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' with specific criteria, including high-end vs. low-end services and pricing structures. The changed text broadly defines it as 'services provided to clients,' omitting these specifics. The contradicted text relies on the original definition to delineate the scope of the non-compete clause. The omission makes it unclear which services are covered by the non-compete, creating a contradiction and making enforcement difficult, as the scope has become ambiguous. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Services" was comprehensive, listing specific types of IT services and explicitly excluding certain activities by CompuCom. The modified definition is extremely vague. This creates an in-text contradiction in Article III.2(a), because it's now difficult to determine which 'Services' CompuCom is restricted from providing to accounts listed on Exhibits B and C. The lack of a clear definition creates significant ambiguity in enforcing the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period CompuCom will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit B hereto; --------- (ii) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit C hereto except to the extent provided for in such Exhibit; or (iii) except as contemplated by the Asset Purchase Agreement, solicit, entice or induce any employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller to terminate his or her employment with Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Services' comprehensively, excluding specific activities by CompuCom. The changed text provides a vague definition. Article III.2(a) restricts CompuCom from providing 'Services' to specific accounts. The lack of a clear definition of 'Services' now makes it difficult to determine which activities CompuCom is restricted from providing, creating a direct conflict and making the non-compete agreement ambiguous and difficult to enforce. This meets the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b).
perturbation.explanation: By removing all conditions under which CompuCom must accept orders, but retaining the base requirement to accept them, the agreement becomes internally contradictory. Section 3.1(b) implies limitations and conditions on this acceptance, but Article IV now states an unconditional acceptance. This makes the intent of both sections unclear and unenforceable. Specifically, the reference in Section 3.1(b) 'in accordance with the provisions of Article IV' is now problematic.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the conditions under which CompuCom must accept orders from the Seller, as outlined in the original text. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Seller will cause customers to procure products from CompuCom 'in accordance with the provisions of Article IV'. Because Article IV no longer contains any provisions or conditions related to order acceptance, this creates an inconsistency, as now the customer has no provisions in Article IV to follow, and since Section 3.1(b) states that the acceptance should be in accordance with provisions in Article IV, and Article IV now does not have those provisions, a contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: 6.5 Specific Performance. The parties acknowledge that money damages -------------------- are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for specific performance or injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof and, to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.5 Specific Performance. The parties acknowledge that money damages -------------------- are an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause stated money damages are NOT an adequate remedy, allowing for specific performance. The modified clause directly contradicts this, stating that money damages ARE an adequate remedy. This eliminates the possibility of seeking specific performance, even though certain breaches (like non-disclosure or non-compete) are inherently difficult to quantify in monetary terms. If other sections rely on the availability of specific performance as an enforcement mechanism, this creates an internal contradiction and undermines the enforceability of the agreement as a whole.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All rights, powers and remedies provided ------------------- for under this Agreement or otherwise available in respect hereof at law or in equity shall be cumulative and not alternative, and the exercise or beginning of the exercise of any thereof by any party shall not preclude the simultaneous or later exercise of any other such right, power or remedy by such party.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 6.5 stated that 'money damages are not an adequate remedy'. This was changed to 'money damages are an adequate remedy'. The contradicted text states that 'all rights, powers and remedies... shall be cumulative and not alternative'. The changed text directly contradicts the original by making money damages adequate. This change removes the availability of other remedies, such as specific performance, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text which states remedies are 'cumulative and not alternative'.

perturbation.original_text: If to CompuCom, to: CompuCom Systems, Inc. 7171 Forest Lane Dallas, Texas 75230 Facsimile No.: (972) 856-5395 Attention: Ms. Lazane Smith, Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer with a copy (which shall not constitute notice to CompuCom) to: Strasburger & Price, L.L.P. 901 Main Street, Suite 4300 Dallas, Texas 75202 Facsimile No.: (214) 651-4330 Attention: Frederick J. Fowler, Esq. or to such other address as such party shall have designated by notice so given to each other party.
perturbation.changed_text: If to CompuCom, to: CompuCom Systems, Inc. 7171 Forest Lane Dallas, Texas 75230 or to such other address as such party shall have designated by notice so given to each other party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the facsimile number and contact person details from the notice provision, and retaining the sentence 'or to such other address as such party shall have designated by notice so given to each other party', it introduces uncertainty. If the other party relies on the specific contact information originally provided and sends a notice via facsimile, it's unclear if that notice is considered valid, since the agreement now lacks this information. This contradiction affects Section 6.1, regarding how notices are given; also, the whole agreement now has an uncertainty issue regarding the validity of a notice given to that original information, but now it is not valid. The sentence 'or to such other address...' now has more weight, since the initial address may not be valid
perturbation.contradicted_text: All notices, requests, demands or other ------------ communications required by or otherwise with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given to any party when delivered personally (by courier service or otherwise), when delivered by facsimile and confirmed by return facsimile, or five business days after being mailed by first-class mail, postage prepaid in each case to the applicable addresses set forth below:
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a facsimile number for sending notices. The changed text removes this information, and the contradicted text states that notices can be delivered by facsimile. This creates a contradiction because the agreement now lacks a specified facsimile number to which notices can be sent, making it unclear if a notice sent by facsimile would be considered valid according to the original terms. This is a strong contradiction as it directly affects the method of delivering notices as outlined in the contradicted text, creating uncertainty about enforceability.
